questions.stem: A nurse finds a conscious patient with type 1 diabetes with a blood glucose of 54 mg/dL and symptoms of shakiness and diaphoresis. The patient is alert and able to swallow. Which is the CORRECT initial treatment?

options.text: Administer glucagon 1 mg subcutaneously
Administer 15 grams of fast-acting carbohydrate orally (e.g., 4 oz juice or glucose tablets) and recheck glucose in 15 minutes
Administer 50 mL of 50% dextrose (D50) IV push
Give the patient a full meal immediately to restore blood glucose

questions.rationale: The '15-15 rule' is the standard for treating conscious, alert patients with mild-to-moderate hypoglycemia who can swallow safely: give 15 grams of rapid-acting carbohydrate (4 oz orange juice, 4 oz regular soda, or 3-4 glucose tablets), wait 15 minutes, and recheck blood glucose. If still < 70 mg/dL, repeat the treatment. This is the safest, most physiologically appropriate approach. Glucagon is reserved for unconscious or unable-to-swallow patients. D50 IV push is reserved for unconscious patients or those without IV access who cannot take oral carbohydrates. A full meal is slower to absorb and can cause a prolonged correction — it is the follow-up after glucose normalizes, not the acute treatment.

distractorRationales.a: "Administer glucagon 1 mg subcutaneously" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "Reserve glucagon for unconscious/unable-to-swallow" raises concern for unstable glucose physiology, so glucose rescue or metabolic stabilization is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.c: The safer response addresses unstable glucose physiology; "Administer 50 mL of 50% dextrose (D50) IV push" moves care toward routine care before glucose rescue or metabolic stabilization.
distractorRationales.d: The option can leave unstable glucose physiology untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "Administer 15 grams of fast-acting carbohydrate orally (e.g., 4 oz juice or glucose tablets) and recheck" fits the safer response to the cue "A nurse finds a conscious patient with type 1 diabetes with a blood glucose of 54 mg/dL".

questions.takeaway: Conscious hypoglycemic patient who can swallow: 15g fast-acting carbs (15-15 rule) → recheck in 15 min → repeat if still low → then a snack with protein. Reserve glucagon for unconscious/unable-to-swallow. Reserve D50 for severe hypoglycemia without oral access.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for four patients on a medical-surgical unit. Which patient should the nurse assess first?

options.text: A 58-year-old with type 2 diabetes who reports feeling 'shaky' and has a blood glucose of 62 mg/dL
A 44-year-old post-appendectomy (day 2) requesting pain medication, pain rated 5/10
A 71-year-old with heart failure who gained 1 lb overnight and is on daily weights
A 33-year-old with a UTI who asks when the next dose of antibiotics is due

questions.rationale: The patient with a blood glucose of 62 mg/dL has symptomatic hypoglycemia ('shaky'), which is an acute, life-threatening condition requiring immediate intervention. Untreated hypoglycemia can rapidly progress to seizures, loss of consciousness, and brain injury. The other patients represent important but lower-acuity concerns: the post-op patient has manageable pain, the heart failure patient's weight gain needs assessment but is not immediately life-threatening, and the UTI patient's antibiotic timing is a scheduled task.

distractorRationales.b: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of glucose rescue or metabolic stabilization. The stem cue "Apply the ABCs and Maslow's hierarchy: acute metabolic emergencies (hypoglycemia, airway compromise) always outrank scheduled pain requests, routine" makes unstable glucose physiology the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.c: The safer response addresses unstable glucose physiology; "A 71-year-old with heart failure who gained 1 lb overnight and is on daily weights" moves care toward diagnostic reassurance before glucose rescue or metabolic stabilization.
distractorRationales.d: The safer response addresses unstable glucose physiology; "A 33-year-old with a UTI who asks when the next dose of antibiotics is due" moves care toward diagnostic reassurance before glucose rescue or metabolic stabilization.

questions.takeaway: Apply the ABCs and Maslow's hierarchy: acute metabolic emergencies (hypoglycemia, airway compromise) always outrank scheduled pain requests, routine monitoring, and informational questions.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A community health nurse is teaching a group of nursing students about levels of prevention. Which example correctly illustrates primary prevention?

options.text: Providing mammography screening to detect early breast cancer in women over 40
Administering the influenza vaccine to a healthy adult before flu season
Prescribing physical therapy for a patient recovering from a hip fracture
Conducting a colonoscopy to identify and remove pre-cancerous polyps

questions.rationale: Primary prevention: actions taken before disease occurs to prevent onset. Examples: immunization, health education, smoking cessation counseling, fluoridated water, condom use. Secondary prevention: early detection of existing disease (screening) — mammography, colonoscopy, blood pressure screening, Pap smear. Tertiary prevention: reducing disability and restoring function in patients with established disease — rehabilitation, cardiac rehab, chronic disease management. Mammography and colonoscopy are secondary prevention (detecting disease before symptoms). Physical therapy post-fracture is tertiary prevention.

distractorRationales.a: The option can leave circulation or perfusion untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "Administering the influenza vaccine to a healthy adult before flu season" fits the safer response to the cue "A community health nurse is teaching a group of nursing students about levels of prevention".
distractorRationales.c: This distractor shifts care toward premature attribution instead of patient teaching and medication-safety reinforcement. The stem cue "A community health nurse is teaching a group of nursing students about levels of prevention" makes circulation or perfusion the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.d: The cue "Physical therapy post-fracture is tertiary prevention" suggests circulation or perfusion, so "Conducting a colonoscopy to identify and remove pre-cancerous polyps" would not correct the active problem. Teaching items should reinforce the specific medication, symptom, or safety rule rather than inventing a new emergency.

questions.takeaway: Primary prevention = before disease (vaccines, education); Secondary = early detection (screening); Tertiary = limit disability/restore function (rehab). Not to be confused with clinical levels of care.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A nurse has four patients to assess. Which patient should the nurse see FIRST, applying Maslow's hierarchy of needs?

options.text: A patient who is anxious about an upcoming procedure
A patient with a respiratory rate of 8 breaths/min and SpO2 of 88%
A patient who needs discharge teaching before going home
A patient asking for pain medication rated 4/10

questions.rationale: Maslow's hierarchy places physiological needs at the foundation. Oxygenation is the highest physiological priority. A patient with RR 8 and SpO2 88% is in respiratory distress — an immediate life-threatening concern that takes absolute priority. Anxiety (A) is a safety/psychological need. Discharge teaching (C) is a self-actualization/cognitive need. Pain rated 4/10 (D) is physiological but not immediately life-threatening compared to respiratory failure.

distractorRationales.a: The option can leave oxygenation or ventilation untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "A patient with a respiratory rate of 8 breaths/min and SpO2 of 88%" fits the safer response to the cue "Oxygenation is the highest physiological priority".
distractorRationales.c: The option can leave oxygenation or ventilation untreated because it moves to activity, intake, or discharge planning too early. "A patient with a respiratory rate of 8 breaths/min and SpO2 of 88%" fits the safer response to the cue "Oxygenation is the highest physiological priority".
distractorRationales.d: The option can leave oxygenation or ventilation untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "A patient with a respiratory rate of 8 breaths/min and SpO2 of 88%" fits the safer response to the cue "Oxygenation is the highest physiological priority".

questions.takeaway: Maslow hierarchy on NCLEX: airway/breathing/circulation (physiological) always come before safety, love/belonging, esteem, and self-actualization.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A nurse is completing an admission assessment on a 79-year-old patient transferred from a nursing facility. Which combination of factors identifies the HIGHEST fall risk?

options.text: Age >65 and female gender
History of fall in the past 6 months, taking 3 antihypertensive medications, and orthostatic hypotension (systolic drop >20 mmHg on standing)
Mild bilateral hearing loss and BMI of 29
Mild arthritis in both knees and occasional evening confusion

questions.rationale: The highest fall risk constellation combines: (1) Prior fall history — the single strongest predictor of future falls in the elderly; (2) Polypharmacy with antihypertensives — alpha-blockers, diuretics, and calcium channel blockers commonly cause orthostatic hypotension and syncope; (3) Documented orthostatic hypotension — directly causes dizziness and falls upon position change. Both the Morse Fall Scale and STRATIFY risk tool weight prior falls most heavily. Key interventions: bed in lowest position, non-skid footwear, call light within reach, scheduled toileting (most falls occur during nighttime toileting), orthostatic vital signs before ambulation, and urgent medication review. Age and gender alone are poor predictors without functional factors.

distractorRationales.a: The safer response addresses medication toxicity; "Age >65 and female gender" moves care toward routine care before medication-safety intervention.
distractorRationales.c: The safer response addresses medication toxicity; "Mild bilateral hearing loss and BMI of 29" moves care toward routine observation before medication-safety intervention.
distractorRationales.d: The option can leave medication toxicity untreated because it normalizes a finding that needs reassessment. "History of fall in the past 6 months, taking 3 antihypertensive medications, and orthostatic hypotension (systolic" fits the safer response to the cue "Highest fall risk: prior fall + polypharmacy (antihypertensives) + orthostatic hypotension".

questions.takeaway: Highest fall risk: prior fall + polypharmacy (antihypertensives) + orthostatic hypotension. Prior fall is the strongest single predictor. Target modifiable risk factors aggressively.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: An RN is caring for four patients and needs to delegate tasks. Which task is appropriate to assign to an unlicensed assistive personnel (UAP/CNA)?

options.text: Assess the breath sounds of a patient with new-onset dyspnea
Administer a tube feeding to a stable patient via an established G-tube
Ambulate a postoperative patient 24 hours after hip replacement surgery
Teach a diabetic patient how to perform foot care

questions.rationale: UAPs can perform tasks that are routine, repetitive, and do not require nursing judgment or assessment. Ambulating a stable postoperative patient 24 hours post-hip replacement is a safe, predictable task for UAP after the RN has assessed the patient's stability. Assessment of breath sounds (A) requires nursing judgment and cannot be delegated. Administering tube feedings (B) — in many states, enteral feeding via established G-tube can be delegated to trained UAPs, but the skill level required is greater than ambulation; this varies by state — ambulation is universally appropriate for CNA delegation. Patient education (D) is an RN responsibility requiring professional judgment.

distractorRationales.a: This distractor shifts care toward premature activity instead of airway and breathing intervention. The stem cue "Patient education (D) is an RN responsibility requiring professional judgment" makes oxygenation or ventilation the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.b: The cue "medication administration remain with the RN" suggests oxygenation or ventilation, so "Administer a tube feeding to a stable patient via an established G-tube" would not correct the active problem. Airway assessment, positioning, oxygen support, or rapid escalation protects ventilation before teaching or documentation.
distractorRationales.d: This distractor shifts care toward premature teaching instead of airway and breathing intervention. The stem cue "UAPs can perform routine, non-assessment tasks: ambulation, ADLs, vital signs on stable patients, I&O — assessment, teaching" makes oxygenation or ventilation the active safety concern.

questions.takeaway: UAPs can perform routine, non-assessment tasks: ambulation, ADLs, vital signs on stable patients, I&O — assessment, teaching, and medication administration remain with the RN.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A patient delivers vaginally and 30 minutes later has an estimated blood loss of 750 mL with a soft, boggy uterus displaced to the right. BP is 94/58, HR 118. Which sequence of interventions is correct?

options.text: IV oxytocin infusion, bimanual uterine compression, empty the bladder, call the provider
Empty the bladder, fundal massage, IV oxytocin infusion, call the provider if no improvement
Call the provider, obtain type and crossmatch, begin blood transfusion
Place in Trendelenburg, administer IV epinephrine, call for hysterectomy consent

questions.rationale: Postpartum hemorrhage (PPH) from uterine atony management: (1) Massage the fundus — stimulates uterine contraction. (2) Empty the bladder — a full bladder displaces the uterus and prevents contraction (uterus displaced to the right = full bladder). (3) IV oxytocin — first-line uterotonic. (4) Notify provider if no response. Bladder emptying is critical and often overlooked — the displaced, boggy uterus signals bladder distension. Epinephrine (D) and hysterectomy are not indicated at this stage.

distractorRationales.a: The cue "BP is 94/58, HR 118" suggests bleeding or anticoagulation injury, so "IV oxytocin infusion, bimanual uterine compression, empty the bladder, call the provider" would not correct the active problem. Bleeding risk requires assessment and source control before comfort measures, teaching, or routine medication timing.
distractorRationales.c: "Call the provider, obtain type and crossmatch, begin blood transfusion" relies on notification without the bedside safety step. The cue "BP is 94/58, HR 118" raises concern for bleeding or anticoagulation injury, so bleeding-risk intervention is needed before waiting for a callback.
distractorRationales.d: The safer response addresses bleeding or anticoagulation injury; "Place in Trendelenburg, administer IV epinephrine, call for hysterectomy consent" moves care toward waiting for a callback before bleeding-risk intervention.

questions.takeaway: Boggy uterus displaced to the right after delivery = full bladder; empty bladder first, then fundal massage + oxytocin.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A post-operative patient on day 2 following abdominal surgery complains of left calf pain, warmth, and swelling. Vital signs are stable. Which action is the NURSE'S PRIORITY?

options.text: Elevate the leg and apply warm compresses
Perform Homan's sign assessment and document the result
Notify the provider and anticipate orders for duplex ultrasound
Administer the scheduled enoxaparin and continue monitoring

questions.rationale: Unilateral calf pain, warmth, and swelling in a post-operative patient are classic signs of deep vein thrombosis (DVT). The nurse's priority is to notify the provider so imaging (duplex ultrasound — gold standard for DVT) can be ordered promptly. Homan's sign (B) is unreliable and has been removed from clinical guidelines. Warmth and elevation (A) delay diagnosis. Administering the next prophylactic dose (D) without a confirmed DVT diagnosis and provider notification is inappropriate.

distractorRationales.a: The safer response addresses medication toxicity; "Elevate the leg and apply warm compresses" moves care toward routine care before medication-safety intervention.
distractorRationales.b: The cue "The nurse's priority is to notify the provider so imaging (duplex ultrasound — gold standard for DVT) can" suggests medication toxicity, so "Perform Homan's sign assessment and document the result" would not correct the active problem. Medication safety depends on checking contraindications, labs, and adverse effects before giving or continuing the drug.
distractorRationales.d: The cue "Administering the next prophylactic dose (D) without a confirmed DVT diagnosis and provider notification is inappropriate" suggests medication toxicity, so "Administer the scheduled enoxaparin and continue monitoring" would not correct the active problem. Medication safety depends on checking contraindications, labs, and adverse effects before giving or continuing the drug.

questions.takeaway: Post-op calf pain + swelling = suspect DVT; notify provider and anticipate duplex ultrasound — Homan's sign is unreliable.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A client with depression says: 'I don't see the point in trying anymore. Nothing I do matters.' Which nurse response is most therapeutic?

options.text: 'I understand. Sometimes things feel hopeless, but you have so much to live for.'
'Tell me more about what you mean when you say nothing matters.'
'Everyone feels that way sometimes. It will get better soon.'
'You shouldn't think like that. Let's focus on the positive things in your life.'

questions.rationale: Option B is an open-ended exploration that invites the client to elaborate, demonstrating active listening without judgment. It also screens for suicidal ideation — 'nothing I do matters' and 'no point in trying' may signal passive suicidal thinking. Option A uses false reassurance ('so much to live for') and assumes understanding without exploring further. Option C minimizes the client's experience ('everyone feels that way'). Option D is non-therapeutic confrontation that dismisses the client's feelings.

distractorRationales.a: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of bedside safety action. The stem cue "therapeutic communication safety" makes clinical safety risk the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.c: The option can leave clinical safety risk untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "'Tell me more about what you mean when you say nothing matters.'" fits the safer response to the cue "therapeutic communication safety".
distractorRationales.d: "'You shouldn't think like that. Let's focus on the positive things in your life.'" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "therapeutic communication safety" raises concern for clinical safety risk, so bedside safety action is needed before routine care.

questions.takeaway: Therapeutic communication: use open-ended statements to explore meaning, especially when statements may signal suicidal ideation.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for a patient with pneumonia who is receiving 4L/min via nasal cannula. The SpO2 is 91%. The nurse repositions the patient and administers the scheduled nebulizer treatment. Which finding would indicate the interventions were effective?

options.text: Respiratory rate decreases from 24 to 18 breaths/min and SpO2 increases to 95%
The patient reports less anxiety and requests to watch television
Adventitious breath sounds increase bilaterally after the nebulizer
The patient's skin color remains unchanged and RR remains at 24/min

questions.rationale: Effective respiratory interventions produce measurable objective improvements in gas exchange: SpO2 rising to ≥ 94-95% and respiratory rate normalizing toward < 20/min indicate improved ventilation and oxygenation. Decreased anxiety may reflect improved oxygenation but is subjective and non-specific. Increased breath sounds after a nebulizer can indicate mobilization of secretions (which is initially normal), not necessarily a therapeutic response. Unchanged SpO2 and RR indicate no meaningful improvement from the interventions.

distractorRationales.b: "The patient reports less anxiety and requests to watch television" treats active deterioration as something safe to watch. The cue "Evaluate respiratory interventions with objective data: SpO2 trend, RR normalization" raises concern for oxygenation or ventilation, so airway and breathing intervention is needed before routine observation.
distractorRationales.c: The safer response addresses oxygenation or ventilation; "Adventitious breath sounds increase bilaterally after the nebulizer" moves care toward routine observation before airway and breathing intervention.
distractorRationales.d: The safer response addresses oxygenation or ventilation; "The patient's skin color remains unchanged and RR remains at 24/min" moves care toward routine care before airway and breathing intervention.

questions.takeaway: Evaluate respiratory interventions with objective data: SpO2 trend, RR normalization, and work of breathing (accessory muscle use, nasal flaring). Subjective comfort is supportive but secondary.
